translate.title: Subtitle Translation
translate.subtitle: Translate SRT files using AI via OpenRouter (GPT, Claude, Google API, Mistral) or local LLM
translate.batchSizeTooltip: Number of subtitles per API request. Higher values = fewer calls but more tokens.
translate.batchSizeHelp:
Controls how many subtitle lines are sent in each API request.

  • 🎯 Precise (5) — Sends just 5 lines per request. Very slow but gives the model maximum focus on each line. Best for small/weak models or when accuracy is critical.
  • ⚖️ Balanced (15) — Good middle ground. Enough context for coherent translation without overwhelming the model.
  • 🚀 Fast (30) — Sends 30 lines at once. Much faster, works well with large models (Google API Pro, GPT-4, etc.).
  • ⚡ Turbo (50) — Maximum batch size. Fewest API calls but uses a lot of context window. Only recommended for top-tier models.

Smaller batches = slower but more reliable. Larger batches = faster but higher risk of errors with weaker models.
translate.contextPlaceholder: Detour (1945): noir road movie set in post-war America. Keep a fatalistic, hard-boiled tone with period slang and terse dialogue; preserve references to class tension, hitchhiking culture, and morally ambiguous characters.
translate.customEndpointHint: Enter an OpenAI-compatible endpoint. The API key is optional if your endpoint doesn't require authentication.
translate.customEndpointRequired: Endpoint URL is required for custom provider
translate.selectFileAndKey: Select a file and an API key
sync.title: Subtitle Synchronization
sync.subtitle: Align subtitles with audio using smart checkpoints
sync.wizard.title: Synchronization Wizard
sync.confirmReset: Are you sure you want to reset the synchronization?
sync.tooltip.loadAudio: Load audio or video file (only audio is used)
sync.audioPlaceholder: Load an audio or video file
sync.audioFormats: Audio: MP3, WAV, OGG, FLAC • Video: MP4, MKV, AVI, WebM
sync.srtPlaceholder: Load an SRT file to start
sync.tooltipLoadSession: Resume a saved sync session (.json). A session stores the SRT file, video path, all confirmed anchors and progress, so you can pick up where you left off.
sync.tooltipSaveSession: Save the current sync state to a .json file. Includes: SRT path, video path, all confirmed anchors and offsets. Useful to pause and resume work later.
sync.tooltipSaveFile: Export the final synchronized SRT file (.srt) with all timing corrections from anchors applied. This is the ready-to-use output file.
sync.dropSrtFirst: Load an SRT file first, then the audio.
sync.needAudioForAnchor: Load an audio file before confirming anchors.
sync.dropFileHint: First the SRT file, then the Audio/Video file (or both)
sync.wizard.allSynced: All subtitles appear to be synchronized!
sync.wizard.suggestSave: The offset is consistent across all checkpoints. You can save the file now.
sync.wizard.selectCheckpoint: Select a subtitle from the list to start
sync.wizardHelp:
The Synchronization Wizard guides you through aligning subtitles to the media timeline.

  • Checkpoint — The wizard picks strategic subtitle lines to validate timing quickly.
  • Offset — Use +/- buttons or ↑/↓ keys to nudge subtitle timing in 100ms steps.
  • Confirm — Press Enter or click the confirm button to save the anchor and jump to the next checkpoint.
  • Replay — Press R to replay the current segment while fine-tuning timing.

When offsets become consistent across checkpoints, synchronization is likely complete and you can save the file.
sync.statusHelp:
Status panel overview:

  • Subtitles — Total subtitle lines loaded from the SRT.
  • Anchors — Confirmed sync points used for interpolation.
  • Average offset — Mean timing correction across anchors.
  • Completion — Progress percentage based on validated checkpoints.
  • Go to line — Jump directly to a subtitle number for manual inspection.
sync.subtitleListHelp:
Subtitle list interactions:

  • Single click — Select the subtitle and move the wizard to that point.
  • Double click — Jump and start playback from that subtitle.
  • Right click — Open context actions (play/go to subtitle).
  • Green rows — Already confirmed anchors.
  • Auto-load — Scrolling loads additional subtitle ranges automatically.
sync.newSyncDesc: Reset and start a new synchronization
sync.helpContent:
Synchronize subtitle timing using audio playback and smart checkpoints.

  1. Load SRT — Open the subtitle file to synchronize
  2. Load Audio/Video — Open the matching audio or video file (only audio is played)
  3. Adjust offset — Listen to the audio at each checkpoint, adjust the offset until the subtitle matches what you hear
  4. Confirm — The wizard automatically jumps to the next smart checkpoint using binary search
  5. Save — When all checkpoints are consistent, save the corrected SRT file

Tip: Use ↑/↓ to adjust offset (auto-replays), R to replay, Enter to confirm. Click any subtitle in the list for manual lookup.
comingSoon.title: Coming Soon
transcribe.title: Audio Transcription
transcribe.subtitle: Generate SRT subtitles from audio/video using Whisper AI
transcribe.whisperModelDesc: Download models for local transcription. Click a model to download and set it as default.
transcribe.modelDownloadNote: This model is not downloaded yet. Please download it before starting transcription.
transcribe.selectFilesFirst: Please select input and output files first
transcribe.wordTimestampsDesc: Millimetric subtitles. Also allows Whisper to split sentences across multiple lines.
transcribe.segmentHelp:
Controls the maximum duration of each subtitle segment produced by Whisper.

  • ✂️ Short (10s) — Many brief subtitle lines. Ideal for language learning (easier to read and review). May split sentences across lines.
  • 📝 Medium (20s) — Balanced approach. Good for most content with moderate speech density.
  • 📄 Standard (30s) — Whisper's default. Natural sentence grouping for most media.
  • 📖 Long (60s) — Fewer, longer subtitle blocks. Good for monologues or narration. May produce very long lines.
transcribe.ffmpegMissingDesc: FFmpeg is required to extract audio from video files. Install it with your package manager.
transcribe.whisperModelHelp:
Choose a Whisper model size. Larger models produce more accurate subtitles but are slower and use more RAM.

ModelSizeSpeedBest for
Tiny~75 MB~32xQuick drafts, clear audio
Base~150 MB~16xGood balance for simple audio
Small~500 MB~6x⭐ Recommended — best accuracy/speed ratio
Medium~1.5 GB~2xNoisy audio, heavy accents
Large~3 GB~1xMaximum accuracy, slow

Icons:

  • ✅ Green check = downloaded and ready
  • ⬇️ Amber arrow = needs to be downloaded first

Tip: Right-click a model for download/uninstall options. Double-click an undownloaded model to start the download.
transcribe.optionsHelp:
Fine-tune how Whisper processes your audio:

  • Source Language — If you know the spoken language, select it for better accuracy. 'Auto-detect' works for most cases but may be less reliable with mixed languages or short clips.
  • Word Timestamps — Generates per-word timing instead of per-phrase. This produces more precise subtitle timing, especially useful for language learning. Recommended: ON
  • Translate to English — Whisper can directly translate non-English speech into English subtitles in a single pass. Useful for quickly generating English subs from foreign-language media.
  • Max Segment Length — Controls the maximum duration (in seconds) of each subtitle line. Shorter values (10-15s) create more frequent, shorter subtitles. Longer values (30-60s) can produce longer, more contextual lines. Default: 30s
transcribe.filesHelp:
Select the audio/video file to transcribe and where to save the generated SRT subtitle file.

Input File

Supported formats: MP4, MKV, AVI, WebM, MP3, WAV, M4A, FLAC, OGG. Audio is automatically extracted from video files using FFmpeg.

Output File

The SRT subtitle file that will be created. By default, it uses the same name and location as the input file with a .srt extension.

💡 Tip: Click on the path text to view/edit it manually, or use the browse button to select via file dialog.
transcribe.newTranscriptionDesc: Clear all fields, logs, and results to start a new transcription
transcribe.comingSoonDesc: Automatic speech-to-text transcription powered by Whisper AI. Generate subtitles directly from audio or video files.
transcribe.feature1: Local Whisper AI processing - no cloud required
transcribe.feature2: Support for multiple languages and accents
flashcards.title: Flashcard Generation
flashcards.subtitle: Create Anki cards from subtitles with audio and images (subs2srs alternative)
flashcards.targetLangSubsDesc: Subtitle file in the original language spoken in the movie or episode
flashcards.nativeLangSubsDesc: Subtitle file in a language you already know, used as translation/reference
flashcards.mediaFileDesc: Video = audio clips + screenshots. Audio = audio clips only
flashcards.subtitleOptionsHelp:
Control how subtitle timing is handled.

  • Use timings from — pick which subtitle track provides the timestamps for media extraction
  • Start / End time — limit generation to a portion of the video (e.g. 0:10:00 → 0:45:00)
  • Time shift — offset timings in milliseconds to fix subtitle sync issues (positive = delay, negative = advance)
  • Combine sentences — merges consecutive lines that end with continuation characters into a single card
flashcards.generateAudioClipsHelp:
Extract an MP3 audio clip for each subtitle line. Requires a media file.

  • Bitrate — audio quality (128 kb/s is a good default)
  • Normalize — even out volume levels across all clips
  • Pad start / end — extend the clip by N milliseconds before and after the subtitle timestamp, so you don't miss the first or last syllable
flashcards.generateSnapshotsHelp:
Capture a screenshot from the video at the midpoint of each subtitle.

  • Width / Height — output image size in pixels (the video frame is scaled to fit)
  • Crop bottom — remove N pixels from the bottom of the frame, useful for hiding hardcoded subtitles burned into the video

Images are saved as JPEG. Requires a video file (not audio-only).
flashcards.generateVideoClipsHelp:
Extract a short video clip for each subtitle line. Gives full audiovisual context on each card.

  • Codec — H.264 (MP4) is widely compatible; MPEG-4 (AVI) is a legacy option
  • Preset — encoding speed vs quality tradeoff (ultrafast = fast but larger files, veryslow = smaller but slow)
  • Video / Audio bitrate — higher = better quality but larger file size
  • Pad start / end — extend the clip window (recommended: 250 ms start, 50 ms end)

⚠ Video clips significantly increase output size. Use only when audio+image isn't enough.
flashcards.namingHelp:
Configure how cards and files are named.

  • Deck name * — used as the Anki deck name and as a prefix for all generated files (e.g. Detour - Italian)
  • First episode number — starting number for the episode tag; useful when processing a series in batches
flashcards.ffmpegMissing: FFmpeg not found. Install FFmpeg to extract audio, snapshots and video clips.
flashcards.dropFileHint: Subtitle files (SRT, ASS, VTT) and media files (MP4, MKV, MP3...)
flashcards.filtersHelp:
Fine-tune which subtitle lines become flashcards.

  • Include / Exclude words — comma-separated keywords to keep or discard lines
  • No duplicates — skip lines whose text already appeared
  • Min / Max chars — reject lines that are too short or too long
  • Min / Max duration — reject lines by their on-screen time (ms)
  • Exclude styled (ASS) — removes lines with override styling tags
  • Only CJK/Kana — keep only lines containing Chinese, Japanese or Korean characters
  • Remove no match — drop Expression lines that have no paired Meaning line
  • Actor filter — keep only lines from specific actors (ASS format)

Filters are applied in order; use Preview to see which lines survive.
flashcards.contextLinesHelp:
Add surrounding subtitle lines to each card for extra context.

  • Leading — number of subtitle lines to include before the current line
  • Trailing — number of subtitle lines to include after the current line
  • Max gap — maximum seconds between adjacent lines to still consider them as context (avoids joining lines from different scenes)

Context lines appear separately on the card, helping you understand the dialogue flow.
flashcards.continuationCharsHint: Characters that indicate a line continues on the next
flashcards.ankiFieldsHelp:
Configure the Anki note type and choose which fields to include in each generated card.

  • Note type name — the Anki note type (model) used for the generated cards.
  • Expression — the subtitle text in the language you are studying
  • Meaning — the subtitle text in the language you already know
  • Audio — [sound:file.mp3] reference
  • Snapshot — <img src="file.jpg"> reference
  • Video — video media reference
  • Tag — an Anki tag for organizing cards (e.g. deck name)
  • Sequence — sequential card number (for ordering)

In TSV mode, fields become columns. In APKG mode, they become note fields in the Anki package.
flashcards.exportFormatHelp:
Choose how the generated flashcards are exported.

TSV + media files

Classic subs2srs format. Produces a .tsv file plus a folder with media. You import the TSV into Anki manually and copy media files to your Anki media folder.

APKG (Anki package)

A single .apkg file containing cards, note type, and all media. Just double-click to import — no manual steps needed.
flashcards.exportTSVDesc: Generates a .tsv file + media folder. Import into Anki manually.
flashcards.exportAPKGDesc: Single .apkg file with everything included. Just double-click to import.
flashcards.cpuCoresNote: At least 1 core stays free for the system. More cores = faster generation.
flashcards.cpuCoresHelp:
Control how many CPU cores to use for flashcard generation.

  • 🍃 Eco — Uses the minimum number of threads (2). System stays fully responsive, but generation is slow. Ideal if you need to use other apps while generating.
  • ⚖️ Balanced — About half your cores. Good middle ground between speed and responsiveness.
  • 🔥 Performance — ~75% of cores (default). Fast generation with acceptable system responsiveness.
  • 💪 Full Power — All available cores (minus 1 for OS). Fastest possible but the system may feel sluggish.
flashcards.filesHelp:
Load subtitle files and choose an output folder.

  • Original subtitle track * — subtitles in the language spoken in the movie or episode
  • Reference translation — subtitles in a language you already know (optional, for bilingual cards)
  • Media file — video or audio source for extracting clips & screenshots
  • Output folder * — where generated files are saved

Automatic file pairing

When you drag & drop or select multiple files, VESTA automatically assigns each file to the correct slot:

  • Role keywords — a file whose name contains native, original, orig or source is assigned as Original subtitle. A file containing translated, translation, reference or ref is assigned as Reference translation.
  • Language codes — if no keyword is found, VESTA checks for a language code at the end of the filename (e.g. movie.ja.srt). It matches it against the Note-type language you selected.
  • Fallback — if neither keywords nor language codes help, the first file (alphabetically) is used as Original subtitle and the second as Reference translation.

Episode ordering (Series mode)

Files are matched across categories (subtitles & media) by episode number, then sorted. Recognised patterns:

  • S01E03, E03, Ep03, Episode 03
  • Separator + number: _03, - 03, .03.

Only consistent episode numbers are used; incidental numbers in the title (e.g. "2001 A Space Odyssey") are ignored.

Tip: use SRT or ASS subtitle files. The tool auto-detects the format.
flashcards.noMediaLoaded: Load a media file to enable audio/video extraction
flashcards.mediaTypeVideo: Video detected — audio clips, screenshots and video clips available
flashcards.mediaTypeAudio: Audio detected — only audio clips available
flashcards.comingSoonDesc: Create Anki flashcards automatically from your subtitles. Perfect for language learning with movies and TV shows.
settings.modelsHint: Click a model to quick-add an API key with this model as default
shortcuts.overlay.title: Keyboard shortcuts
shortcuts.overlay.hint: Press Shift+? to toggle this panel
settings.googleProviderTip: A single Google key works for all Google API models. Choose the specific model in the Translation tab.
settings.localProviderTip: No API key required for local models. Just enter the server endpoint.
settings.groqProviderTip: Ultra-fast inference powered by Groq LPU. Requires a gsk_ API key from console.groq.com.
settings.customProviderTip: Connect any OpenAI-compatible endpoint (OpenRouter, Together, etc.). Configure URL and optional API key.
flashcards.newGenerationDesc: Reset and start a new flashcard generation
translate.optionsHelp:
Configure the AI translation settings:

  • Provider — Choose between Google API, OpenAI, or a local LLM server
  • Model — Select the specific AI model to use
  • Source/Target language — Set the languages for translation
  • Batch size — Number of subtitle lines sent per API call (larger = faster, but may reduce quality)
  • Context window — Surrounding lines included for better contextual translation

Tip: Use a larger context window for dialogue-heavy content to maintain consistency.
translate.filesHelp:
Select input and output subtitle files:

  • Input — The SRT file to translate
  • Output — Where the translated SRT will be saved

The output path is auto-generated from the input filename with a language suffix.
provider.google.desc: Google API native API (requires AIza... key)
provider.groq.desc: Ultra-fast LPU inference — Llama 3.3 70B, GPT-OSS 120B, Qwen 3 32B
settings.errorEndpointRequired: API endpoint is required for local server
settings.errorInvalidGoogleKey: Google API keys should start with 'AIza...'
settings.errorInvalidGroqKey: Groq API keys should start with 'gsk_...'
settings.errorDuplicateCustomName: A custom provider with this name already exists
settings.cardTemplatesDesc: Customize the HTML and CSS templates for exported flashcards
settings.cardStyleDesc: Edit front, back HTML and CSS styling
settings.fieldsDesc: Customize field names and note type
settings.noteTypeNameDesc: The Note Type name used in Anki. If you customize the template, you may want a custom name.
settings.availableVarsInfo: These placeholders are replaced with the actual field values when Anki renders the card. Use them in Front/Back HTML to display content.
settings.cardFrontHtmlInfo: The front side of the flashcard shown during review. This is what you see first and try to recall the answer from.
settings.cardBackHtmlInfo: The back side revealed after you flip the card. Usually contains the answer, audio, images and additional info.
settings.cardCssInfo: CSS styling applied to both front and back of the card. Controls fonts, colors, layout and appearance.
transcribe.inputPathDesc: Enter or edit the path to the audio/video file.
transcribe.outputPathDesc: Enter or edit the output SRT file path.
flashcards.noFilesAdded: No files added yet. Start by adding the original subtitle track.
flashcards.outputPerEpisodeDesc: Generates one APKG file for each episode
flashcards.outputSingleApkgDesc: Combines all episodes into one single APKG
flashcards.mergingApkg: Merging episode packages into a single APKG...

sync.videoCodecHint: Missing video/audio codecs. On Linux install: gstreamer1.0-plugins-good gstreamer1.0-plugins-bad gstreamer1.0-plugins-ugly gstreamer1.0-libav
sync.tooltipOffset: Fine-tune subtitle timing. Positive values delay subtitles, negative values show them earlier.
sync.tooltipConfirmAnchor: Mark the current subtitle as a sync point (Enter). Anchors are reference points used to interpolate nearby subtitle timing.
sync.needVideoForAnchor: Load a video before confirming anchors.
